era_context: The mid-14th century: Europe in the aftermath of the Black Death (1347–1353), experiencing labour shortages, social upheaval, and the early stages of the Renaissance in Italy. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continues. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. China is in the early Ming Dynasty (established 1368, but Zhu Yuanzhang's forces are consolidating control). The Timurid Empire dominates Central Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; oral traditions not systematically recorded in European archives)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Southeast Asia (some chronicle material exists but is underrepresented in Western scholarship)

events.title: Battle of Auray — Breton Succession War
events.description: The decisive engagement in the War of the Breton Succession, fought between Jean de Montfort (supported by England) and Charles of Blois (supported by France). Montfort's forces, led by the English knight John Chandos, defeated Blois's army. Charles of Blois was killed in the battle. This victory secured Montfort's claim to the Duchy of Brittany and represented a significant English success in the broader Hundred Years' War context, though Brittany remained semi-independent.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in near-contemporary chronicles. The date (29 September 1364) is secure in historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Follows from 1341-001 (War of Breton Succession begins)
Part of broader Hundred Years' War context

events.title: Death of John II of France; Accession of Charles V
events.description: King John II of France died in captivity in London (8 April 1364), where he had been held as a hostage following his capture at the Battle of Poitiers (1356). His son Charles V succeeded him. Charles V's reign (1364–1380) marked a significant shift in French strategy during the Hundred Years' War, moving away from pitched battles toward more cautious, economically-focused warfare. Charles V's accession also initiated a period of cultural patronage and administrative reform.
key_figures: John II of France (died in captivity)
events.certainty_note: The death date and succession are well-documented in multiple near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang Consolidates Control in Southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, founder of the Ming Dynasty, continued his military campaigns to eliminate rival warlords and consolidate control over southern China. By 1364, his power base in the Yangtze River valley was secure, and he was preparing for the final push against the Yuan Dynasty in the north. This year marks a critical phase in the transition from Yuan to Ming rule, though the formal proclamation of the Ming Dynasty would come in 1368.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation is well-established, but specific events within 1364 are less precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Timur (Tamerlane) Campaigns in Persia and Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) continued his military campaigns to consolidate control over Central Asia and Persia. By 1364, he had defeated most rival khans and was establishing the Timurid Empire. His campaigns were characterized by rapid cavalry movements, siege warfare, and the destruction of cities that resisted. This period laid the foundation for the Timurid cultural and political dominance that would define the region for the next century.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, r. 1369–1405, but consolidating power in 1364)

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (Book of Victories)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Timur's rise is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 1364 are difficult to date precisely. Most detailed accounts were written decades or centuries later.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad I, continued its expansion into the Balkans. By 1364, Ottoman forces had captured Adrianople (Edirne), which became a new capital and a base for further expansion into European territories. This marked a critical shift in Ottoman strategy, moving from Anatolia into Europe and establishing the foundation for Ottoman dominance in the region for the next five centuries.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Adrianople is traditionally dated to 1362–1365; 1364 is a reasonable approximation within this range. Ottoman sources are often retrospective and less precise on dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Auray (1364-001) is sometimes overstated in English historiography as a major English victory in the Hundred Years' War. However, modern scholarship notes that Brittany's independence was not fully secured by this battle alone, and the broader strategic impact on the war was limited. The battle is better understood as a local success within a larger, protracted conflict. Additionally, the dating of Ottoman expansion into Adrianople (1364-005) is contested; some sources place it in 1362, others in 1365. The traditional narrative of a single 'capture' may oversimplify a gradual process of Ottoman consolidation.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1364 is uneven. European events (particularly those involving England and France) are well-attested in chronicles by Froissart and others, though these sources are not contemporary and reflect court biases. Ottoman and Central Asian events are documented in later compilations that are often retrospective and less precise on dating. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are comprehensive but were compiled after the fact. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Southeast Asia have minimal documentary evidence for this specific year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Auray resolves succession dispute that emerged from broader Hundred Years' War context
Charles V's accession follows from John II's capture at Poitiers
Consolidation of southern China leads to Ming Dynasty proclamation
Ottoman expansion into Balkans leads to Battle of Kosovo
